item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're thinking of Harsh Noise, and even then only the really good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There really isn't anything of substance here. While a general lack of narrative is somewhat to be expected from a Series I SCP, this really feels half-baked, and could most definitely do with a discovery addendum or something similar. This article could have really gone with elaborating on the origin and reasoning of this anomaly's existence, but at the moment this really is a lackluster article with close to no development. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to perform the music have resulted in a disagreeable cacophony, with each instrumental part having no correlation or harmony with the other instruments.

So dubstep, then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can easily be destroyed.
Its just not a threat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These ones where its something that's been found and they're initially unsure of its exact properties, I'd like to see more of the scientific process they went through to establish the rules that govern its effects.

My immediate thought for the next step would be to extract an amount of blood from a D Class before they entered the containment, and arm them with that to see if they then wrote anything without the associated mutilation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, this SCP is annoying me. Everything written here is great, but it needs to be like five times longer and to go into much more detail. Still, I gave it a +1 in account for how i did enjoy it and know it was written back when the standards for the main list were looser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
012, unfinished work.
I really like this one, just the way it is.
Yep. You read that right.
Many people think that the point of 012 is that it makes you go insane. It’s not. The point is to get people to try and finish it. A piece of recorded music, desperately wanting to be finished and violently forcing people around it to give their lives doing so. Why? The music it creates doesn’t even make sense.
But that’s the point. It’s short, simple, and carries an interesting mystery. Just look at all the people in this discussion page giving theories as to what it is, who made it, why it does what it does. If you removed all mention of insanity and psychosis, and just said that it forced people to try and complete it, you’d have the same scip with less questionable medical science.
Sure, I’d like to know if it correlates to any genre, or if it’s proximity or sight that triggers the effect, and maybe just switch around the text to make it less awkward, but even so, I think that this is a good, short scip.
Also, hi to all the CB fans that only just got into the site!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP, but I feel like it needs to be expanded upon to be considered good. Like some cross-SCP testing, like 682 or an SCP that doesn’t have blood or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not nearly enough details. This honestly feels like some one just posted their rough idea that they dreamt about or something. It a piece of music so you think the first thing they would try to do is play it. Why not detail the process they went through to even be able to look at the page? Then a photocopy or even a transcript would affect the quality 100 fold. What happens when they play it using different variables? Octaves? Instruments? Volume? Then there's experiments with other SCP's which I love seeing in articles but, in most cases, it seems like those are relegated to fan-fiction. It's just really disappointing that this one is so shoddy because it's one of the handful that are being featured in SCP: Containment Breach so it must be getting a fair amount of views nowadays. Improving this should really be prioritized so we can give out newcomers a good first impression.

item.title: Re: My lovely work of music
item.content:encoded:
Also it's written in multiple peoples blood. If you're going to be that guy at least actually read the article.

This one is like three paragraphs long so it isn't hard.

item.title: So something I just realized.
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the shortest SCPs and not exactly in a good way.
Sure it's old but many think it's aged poorly.
And people want to expand it, except there's not much to expand on and it would be nonsensical.

Now where have I heard of a document that makes people want to expand it for no reason before realizing it just creates more nonsense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybebtimes WERE different back then… But this really is the example of a lazy article.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Writer of Cacohpony, as a general note, roleplaying is not allowed in the forums. This is noted in the required reading.

item.title: My lovely work of music
item.content:encoded: The music sheet is what I wrote! I wrote it with my own blood! Don’t you get it now?!

item.title: holy mountain mama shit
item.content:encoded: Can we just get an SCP-012/SCP-035 crossover tale already?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't written to emphasize it, but this is scary in a lot of more subtle ways.

1) It's very similar to SCP-140, which makes me think that it can be finished and that something really horrible would happen if it were.

2) This:

SCP-012 was retrieved by Archaeologist K.M. Sandoval during the excavation of a northern Italian tomb destroyed in a recent storm.

SCP-035 was found in a sealed crypt in an abandoned house in Venice, in 18██.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This sucks.

It's also strangely compelling.

Bottom line, if this was the first SCP I ever read, I would be blown away. Without a doubt, I would start searching out more articles.

So for that reason, I'm giving it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hasn't aged well at all

That's an understatement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, while I'll admit that this definitely hasn't aged well at all, I still find it to be a guilty pleasure because it's the first article that really got me invested in the Foundation. So no-vote I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it ever says what 012's effects are.

It seems to state the effects as observations of things that happened, rather than giving information straight as if it were obtained through empirical observation of multiple experimental trials.

Not a great way to present the info, but since the article is so short and it's been around for several years, /shrugs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't care that this is Series 1. I still don't like this. It's just a thing that makes you go crazy and kill yourself. Maybe the Mount Golgotha tales are good, but I really don't like this. Also I don't know if I read this too quickly, but I don't think it ever says what 012's effects are.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: any relation to the music of eric zahn?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I would've enjoyed this SCP a bit more if it explained what would happen to people affected by this SCiP who are then removed from the presence of the SCP, like would it start bothering the removed individual to the point he couldn't return to a normal life anymore or would SCP-012's effects just vanish? I know this is an old SCiP and probably won't get revisited by the author but I really like the concept and I feel like it would've been better with a bit more detail on the mental effects of this SCiP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: shouldn't this be tagged "on-mount-golgotha" ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if is so, then why wouldn't there be research done with the copies?

I down-voted because the idea is great, but the execution is mediocre. Could have been better written back then, or at least updated with interactions with other SCPs.

I'd love to be proactive and submit an update for evaluation or something, but I'm way too newbie in here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably copies or partial scores for a particular instrument don't have the same properties as the original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's one thing that really bothers me about this setup.

Attempts to perform the music have resulted in a disagreeable cacophony, with each instrumental part having no correlation or harmony with the other instruments.

How, exactly? If you look at it, it messes with you.

Maybe if it's specifically looking at or reading to the current "end" that does it…

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: I think just the tag to On Mount Golgotha would suffice. The link to the K.M. tale would be redundant.

item.title: Disregard Post
item.content:encoded: Timer expired - not permitted to crosslink article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thing is, 12-tone isn't atonal or random; it's an extremely complex, rule-based-yet-free-form system, similar to the blues or jazz…and like Harsh Noise or Industrial, the art and beauty is in the whole, not the parts. Take this Schoenburg piece, for instance: it only sounds like chaos until the patterns start to crystallize, and then it becomes something hauntingly beautiful, with fractal complexity and depth. (The walls of sound between ~4:00 and 6:00… gorgeous.)

Personally, I imagine On Mount Golgatha to be written in something like a mix of a raga and a mode, but with lots of semi-dissonant notes thrown in, so that the entire piece is that moment of anticipatory dissonance just before a phrase resolves. It's like a musical equivalent of Pi: there's an obvious formula to it, but you can go on forever trying calculate the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to perform the music have resulted in a disagreeable cacophony, with each instrumental part having no correlation or harmony with the other instruments.

Atonal music? (eg Schoenberg?)

But yeah this article pretty much sums up what many people think of twelve-tone/atonal music.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To this day, surviving subjects swear they could hear Iggy Azalea's voice as they approached SCP-012

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, if this isn't going to get rewritten, maybe it could stand to have an in-line link to the On Mount Golgotha canon? That would help flesh things out, at the very least, and direct attention towards a series that IMO deserves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the more relevant question is "Is it actually possible to finish it?"

It's possible that the piece has no end. So trying to complete it is just a waste of D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why should they? Should it be finished?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why can't the foundation just send in D-Class personnel to "finish" it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it should have the "meta" tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might I recommend The Journal of K. M. Sandoval?

Then check out the On Mount Golgotha Hub for more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is interesting, I think the only thing that would make it more interesting is maybe a document or two, perhaps an attempted interview with someone that has had contact with 012 but was removed before mutilation.

item.title: Lavender Town and other musics that go bump in the night
item.content:encoded:
Welp.
That creepy pasta about children commiting suicide after listining to the song.
Then there is "Gloomy Sunday" a song composed by Rezső Seress which also caused people to commit suicide by then. its pretty intresting when I hear some similarities between this SCP and other urban legends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you asked.

I posit an answer in Bigger Than Jesus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need to ask something. EUCLID! HOW IS THIS THING EUCLID! It doesn't mention anything that would make it a Euclid object. This definitely need a rewrite DESPERATELY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably the only thing keeping this one afloat is it's inclusion in CB. Desperately needs a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The funny thing is the title refers both to the object and the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In every case, the subjects mutilated themselves in order to use their own blood to finish > the piece, resulting in subsequent symptoms of psychosis and massive trauma.

Should've given em' a pen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have been working on a tool for generating such a picture. You can see a sample of the results at http://imgur.com/QLU4u9D if you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, I think it could use a little more detail though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's not quite an upvote from me, I do appreciate this for what it is: a Lovecraftian prop. And that works great as part of the metanarrative. It could be improved, certainly, but it'd have to be handled with silk gloves in order not to have it completely abandon its niche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a solid case for the notion of "less is more." I'd much rather see it stay as-is with enough parts detail and mystery, than see it embellished to the point of losing the current 'feel'. Not saying that anyone is allowed to change it without the author's permission, but I've at least conveyed my thoughts.
Just thought I'd mention it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That has always been the thing for me regarding this piece. The cacophony produced by playing SCP-012 is there on purpose—it means something. There was something done with SCP-1301 playing a part of SCP-012, but I always imagined that there is a work that needs to be finished, the cacophony is part of the point, and there is an actual coda to SCP-012.

(Also, my headcanon is that centuries ago, 035 commissioned 012, promising to pay the composer a king's ransom "when it is finished.")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I get home, I'm writing a tale on this, because you found the only thing about this article that makes me interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This post made me really rethink this SCP. Changing downvote to novote, may even upgrade to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this whole post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of surprised to find a fellow music enthusiast criticizing this one on the composition side. Simple harmony and unified melody are the lowest form of music, after all. There are plenty of examples of intentionally chaotic or dissonant music: Wagner, Beethoven, Avant-Garde Classical pieces like The Rite of Spring, polyphonic Eastern drone styles like Nhã Nhạc, Freeform Jazz, Industrial, Avant-Garde Metal, Punk Rock, Grindcore, and hard Dubstep all come to mind as examples of how completely ignoring the standard western template for music can be used to great artistic effect.

I would float the idea that a skilled composer of any of these schools might be able to resist or even complete SCP-012. Your average person led by what sounds right, or a traditional composer working from deeply ingrained rules, would likely seek to resolve the discordance. (Thus declaring it "impossible to complete" upon discovering that it never does resolve) Someone deeply comfortable with the idea of "aesthetically moving" and "pleasant to experience" being two different things to the point of doing the same thing themselves, however, might be able to hold onto their sanity long enough to complete the work.

Art does not have to be pretty. Some of the most powerful art is intended to invoke feelings like sorrow, anger, despair, pain, frustration, and confusion. I suspect that the composer may have accomplished a little too well exactly the task they set out to do when writing a piece entitled "On Mount Golgotha"

Edit: the more I think about this one, the more I'd like to throw my hat in the ring if it ever gets the update treatment it needs. I'd focus on the "memetic anti-music-theory" aspect. I think I can also possibly justify the euclid aspect by suggesting a connection to the spawning of the chaotic music forms I've mentioned: it has to be moved around occasionally because nearby musicians start subconsciously rebelling against genre conventions and incorporating bits of it into their songs, which tend to be hits that you can't explain your fondness for. (Due to people responding to the watered down snippet.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept and all, but the whole thing feels kinda unfinished. I don't really know what it needs but it just feels like it could use more detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not my cup of tea either. A musical SCP would be good, if well-written by someone who'd know about music. I agree with Corsair Caruso above: what kind of music is it? What makes it cacophonic? Written in blood and maddening is all well and good, but far from enough to make the SCP interesting and original!
Downvoted very hard.

item.title: Re: Interesting article, might take up an edit in the future
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I do know that, though thank you for reminding me. Sorry for the breach of etiquette. I'm still trying to get all the rules for the site under my belt.

Let me amend my previous suggestion to: I think the following changes might be an improvement upon the article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is dumb. I got literally nothing from this. I'm not entertained. I'm not amused. I'm actually kind pissed that this is even on the site. Never have I been more disappointed with a successful Series I. Like, I see the appeal of some successful articles I don't like, but this has no appeal. 140 does everything this does, but better, and 140 actually makes sense. Downvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a perfect piece, an example to show how you write an SCP.
